title: Near-identical photons from distant quantum dot-cavity devices
authors: Thibaut Pollet, Victor Guilloux, Duc-Duy Tran, Anton Pishchagin, Stephen Wein, Joseph A. Sulpizio, William Hease, Petr Stepanov, Petr Steindl, Nico Margaria, Samuel Mister, Martina Morassi, Aristide Lemaître, Thi Huong Au, Sébastien Boissier, Pascale Senellart
abstract: Scalable optical quantum technologies require interference between large numbers of indistinguishable single-photons emitted by independent sources. Semiconductor quantum dots are known to be excellent on-demand sources of single-photons. They show record efficiency when inserted into optical cavities to control their spontaneous emission and generate trains of near identical photons over microsecond timescales. However, generating perfectly identical photons from distant cavity-based sources has remained a long-standing challenge. It requires precise matching of the emission wavelengths and emission dynamics, while simultaneously minimizing spectral noise across all time scales for distant emitters in uncorrelated environments. Here, we report on the nanofabrication of a large number of quantum dot-cavity sources with ultra-low spectral noise and wavelength dispersion. The high source efficiency and the use of two tuning mechanisms enable precise optimization of the spectral overlap between distant sources. With this approach, we demonstrate a two-photon indistinguishability of $88\pm1$ % between photons emitted from two distant sources. Remarkably, this value reaches the upper bound set by the intrinsic indistinguishability of photons emitted successively by each source. These results represent a key milestone for scaling photon-based quantum technologies.
summary: This paper reports on the successful creation of near-identical photons from two separate, distant quantum dot-cavity devices. By using a dual-tuning approach involving both strain and electric fields, the researchers were able to overcome wavelength mismatches and spectral noise. They achieved a high mutual indistinguishability of 88%, which is nearly the maximum possible value allowed by the individual sources. This achievement is a significant milestone for scaling up photonic quantum technologies and building large-scale quantum networks.
main_problem: Achieving high-visibility quantum interference (indistinguishability) between single photons emitted from spatially distant and independent semiconductor quantum dot-cavity sources.
main_result: Demonstrated a record mutual indistinguishability of 88 +/- 1% between two distant sources, reaching the upper bound set by the intrinsic performance of the individual emitters.
method: The researchers used a combination of strain-tuning (via piezo-driven fiber) and electrical tuning (via PIN diode Stark shift) to align wavelengths, followed by Hong-Ou-Mandel interference measurements.
model_or_system: The platform consists of two independent InGaAs quantum dot-cavity micropillars embedded in a PIN diode structure, cooled to 5 K, using longitudinal acoustic (LA) phonon-assisted excitation.
key_observables: Two-photon indistinguishability (M12), Hong-Ou-Mandel visibility (V_HOM), second-order correlation function (g2(0)), and spectral noise/fluctuations.
important_parameters: Indistinguishability of 88%, cavity Q factor of 8000, charge noise at 4.8% of natural linewidth, and photon collection efficiency between 15.6% and 27.5%.
main_assumptions: The calculation of the theoretical upper bound assumes perfect temporal overlap (s12 = 1) between the two photons.
figures_summary: Fig 1: SEM images and fabrication details showing wavelength/cavity distribution and low charge noise. Fig 2: Principles of phonon-assisted excitation and lifetime/Purcell effect measurements. Fig 3: Extraction of mutual indistinguishability and comparison to theoretical bounds. Extended Data: Fabrication process, g2(0) measurements, and optical setup schematics.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the challenge of remote interference, describes the fabrication and tuning architecture, presents the characterization of individual source stability, demonstrates the successful interference of distant sources, and concludes with the scalability and future potential of the approach.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and quantum networks as it demonstrates a scalable method to bridge the gap between single-source performance and remote-source interference, a prerequisite for photon-mediated entanglement in quantum computing.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Fabrication of low-noise identical single-photon sources. a. Scanning electron microscope image of three micropillar single-photon sources. b. Wavelength distribution of fabricated micropillar cavities, fitted to a normal distribution yielding a FWHM of 94 ± 2 pm. The wavelength distribution of the QDs embedded in the micropillars shows a larger standard deviation requiring post-fabrication tuning to bring the QDs into resonance with their respective cavities. c. Spectral distri- bution of the QD ensemble under above-band excitation for two wafers with different QD areal densities, fitted with Gaussian functions. FIG. 2. Individual performance of the two QD-cavity single-photon sources. a. Working principle of the LA phonon-assisted excitation scheme. b. Charge plateau of the negative trion X−state for the two QDs embedded in the separate micropillars S1 and S2. The dashed lines represent the cavity resonance of each micropillar. c. Lifetimes of S1 and S2 measured at different wavelengths by varying the PIN bias. The data points are fitted with a Lorentzian function to model the detuning-dependent Purcell effect. FIG. 3. Two-photon interference between remote QDs embedded in two separate micropillar cavities. a. Illustration of the HOM interference setup, including the motorized HWP placed in one of the optical paths to switch between orthogonal (⊥) and parallel (∥) polarization configurations (see Extended Data Fig. 3 for more information). b. Remote-source indistinguishability as a function of emitter wavelengths, measured by varying the PIN bias voltage of each sources. At each pair of wavelengths, the photon correlations in the ⊥and ∥configurations are recorded for 1 s. We plot the visibility extracted using Eq. 10 and corrected for setup imperfections (Methods).
